h1Title: Cyclic Neutropenia: Symptoms, Treatment & Life Management
heroOverview: Cyclic neutropenia (CN, OMIM 162000) is a rare bone marrow failure disorder characterised by regular oscillations in the production of all granulocyte cell lines, producing a predictable cycle of severe neutropenia approximately every 21 days (range 14-35 days), with each nadir lasting 3-5 days. The disorder is caused by autosomal dominant germline mutations in the ELANE gene (19p13.3), encoding neutrophil elastase, a key enzyme in neutrophil differentiation. Approximately 2,000-5,000 people are affected worldwide, with an estimated incidence of 1 in 1,000,000 live births. During neutropenic nadirs, patients are vulnerable to bacterial and fungal infections, oral ulceration, fever, and fatigue. Between nadirs, neutrophil counts may be normal or mildly reduced. The condition is distinguished from severe congenital neutropenia (SCN) by its periodic pattern and generally milder course. Subcutaneous G-CSF (filgrastim or lenograstim) is the standard treatment, converting the unpredictable cycle into a manageable chronic condition with dramatically reduced infection burden.

keyStats.prevalence: 1 in 1,000,000 live births; estimated 2,000-5,000 patients worldwide; autosomal dominant with variable penetrance
keyStats.demographics: Affects both sexes equally; diagnosis typically in childhood but may be delayed; reported in all ethnic groups
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 2-6 years; many patients diagnosed in first year of life when pattern becomes apparent
keyStats.globalCases: Rare; most data from European and North American cohorts; likely underdiagnosed in low-resource settings where automated neutrophil counts are less available

definition: Cyclic neutropenia (ICD-10: D70.4, acquired neutropenia / cyclic neutropenia) is an autosomal dominant bone marrow failure disorder characterised by oscillations in the production of granulocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils) and monocytes with a periodicity of approximately 21 days. ELANE gene mutations (most commonly p.S97L, p.P139L, p.G185R) cause misfolded neutrophil elastase protein to accumulate in the endoplasmic reticulum of myeloid precursors, triggering the unfolded protein response and apoptosis of developing granulocytes. This leads to a cyclical pattern where neutrophil counts fall from normal (2,000-7,000/µL) to severely neutropenic (<200/µL) over 3-5 days, remain low for 3-5 days during the nadir, then recover over 5-7 days before the cycle repeats. During nadirs, the monocyte count and platelet count may also fall, reflecting shared multilineage haematopoietic progenitor involvement. CN is distinguished from SCN by its periodic pattern (SCN has persistently low neutrophils) and from secondary neutropenia by its predictable cycling. The condition is typically stable throughout life with no risk of progression to MDS or AML, unlike SCN.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, caused by autosomal dominant ELANE mutations. Characteristic 21-day cycles with severe neutropenia nadirs and associated symptoms. Most mutations affect the protease domain of neutrophil elastase.

typesClassification.type: Mild cyclic neutropenia with partial penetrance
typesClassification.description: Some ELANE mutation carriers have milder cycles or are asymptomatic; neutrophil counts may oscillate without clinically significant nadirs.
Rare variant where ELANE mutations co-segregate with WAS gene variants, producing a modified cyclic pattern. Distinct from classic ELANE CN.
Very rare cases where long-standing CN develops additional somatic mutations; requires monitoring for rare progression to MDS/AML.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent oral ulcers (aphthous stomatitis), gingivitis, and painful mucosal lesions appearing during the neutropenic nadir.
Fever above 38°C occurring predictably with each cycle nadir, often without an identifiable infectious source but frequently associated with bacterial infection.
Severe fatigue and malaise during neutropenic nadirs, often beginning 1-2 days before the neutrophil count falls.
Recurrent bacterial infections during nadirs: skin and soft-tissue infections (boils, cellulitis), respiratory infections (sinusitis, otitis media, pneumonia), and perirectal infections.
Recurrent upper respiratory tract infections with nasal congestion, sinus pain, and purulent nasal discharge during each cycle.
Headaches and general malaise preceding each nadir by 1-2 days.
Abdominal pain and nausea during nadirs from neutropenic gut inflammation.
Joint pain and myalgia occurring with each cycle.
Weight loss and reduced appetite during periods of frequent infections.
Recurrent otitis media with hearing difficulty in children with CN.
earlyWarningSigns: Regular cyclical pattern of fever, oral ulcers, or infections occurring every 2-5 weeks
Neutropenia documented on a complete blood count performed during a symptomatic episode
Three or more unexplained fever episodes in a child over 3 months without obvious infectious trigger
Recurrent aphthous ulcers with predictable periodicity
Unexplained recurrent bacterial infections in an otherwise healthy child
Family history of cyclic neutropenia or ELANE mutation
emergencySigns: Fever above 38.5°C with neutropenia (<500/µL) — requires urgent assessment and IV antibiotics for presumed bacterial infection
Severe perirectal pain and inflammation suggesting neutropenic proctitis — requires urgent antibiotics and surgical review
Pneumonia with hypoxia in a neutropenic patient — requires urgent hospital admission and broad-spectrum antibiotics
Severe oral ulceration with dysphagia and dehydration — requires IV fluids and nutritional support
Sepsis with hypotension and organ dysfunction in neutropenic phase — requires emergency ICU-level care

causes.description: Heterozygous missense mutations in the ELANE gene (19p13.3) cause CN in over 90% of cases. The most common mutations are p.S97L (c.290C>T), p.G185R, and p.P139L. These mutations cause misfolding of neutrophil elastase, accumulation in the ER of myeloid precursors, and activation of the unfolded protein response leading to apoptosis of developing granulocytes. The autosomal dominant inheritance means affected individuals have a 50% chance of passing the mutation to each child.

causes.cause: Cyclical pattern of granulocyte production disruption
causes.description: The pathophysiology involves the intrinsic hematopoietic oscillator (the 'neutrophil clock'), a cell-intrinsic timer in granulocyte-monocyte progenitors that controls the release of mature cells into the bloodstream. ELANE mutations disrupt this oscillator, causing synchronized waves of neutrophil production followed by depletion and recovery. The cycle length is regulated by the intrinsic timing mechanism and is relatively consistent within individuals over time.

The oscillator affects all granulocyte lineages (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils) and often monocytes. Platelet counts may fall during some nadirs. This multilineage pattern distinguishes CN from isolated neutropenia.

While the cycle is intrinsic to the oscillator, external factors can modulate nadir timing and severity: infections, stress, and inflammatory cytokines can precipitate earlier or more severe nadirs. Conversely, G-CSF therapy synchronises and stabilises the cycle.

Neutrophils in CN patients, even during recovery phases, have functional abnormalities including reduced granule content, impaired chemotaxis, and reduced bactericidal activity. This explains why some infections occur even during neutrophil recovery.

riskFactors.description: Heterozygous ELANE mutation is the primary risk factor. Nearly 100% penetrance for the CN phenotype in those with confirmed pathogenic mutations.
Autosomal dominant inheritance; affected parents have a 50% risk of transmitting the mutation. Up to 50% of cases are inherited from an affected parent.
Approximately 50% of cases arise from de novo mutations with no family history. Newborns of unaffected parents with a de novo mutation will have CN.
Symptoms typically begin in the first 2 years of life; diagnosis is often delayed until the pattern becomes clear over several cycles.
Viral infections can trigger earlier or more severe nadirs by stressing the bone marrow and suppressing granulopoiesis.

affectedDemographics: Affects males and females equally (autosomal inheritance).
Most diagnoses made in early childhood when cycle pattern becomes apparent after repeated infections.
All ethnic groups affected; slight female predominance in some series.
Adults may present undiagnosed with a long history of recurrent infections.
The condition is lifelong; symptoms may attenuate in some elderly patients.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of CN is based on clinical history (recurrent infections or fevers occurring at regular intervals), serial complete blood count monitoring, and genetic testing. To establish the diagnosis, CBC with differential must be performed 2-3 times per week for 6-8 weeks to capture at least 2 complete cycles and demonstrate the characteristic oscillation between normal and severely neutropenic counts. A nadir neutrophil count below 200/µL occurring at regular intervals of approximately 21 days confirms the diagnosis. ELANE gene sequencing confirms the genetic basis and distinguishes CN from SCN. Bone marrow examination is not routinely required but may show the characteristic 'maturation arrest' at the myelocyte-promyelocyte stage during nadirs. CN must be distinguished from SCN (persistent severe neutropenia), drug-induced neutropenia (temporal relationship to drug exposure), and benign ethnic neutropenia (normal variant in certain populations without symptoms).

diagnosticTests.test: Serial complete blood counts (2-3x per week for 6-8 weeks)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the cyclical neutrophil pattern; neutrophil counts oscillate between normal (>2,000/µL) and severely neutropenic (<200/µL) every 21 days
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Regular blood tests; patient or parent records dates to correlate with symptoms; pattern typically emerges after 2-3 cycles

diagnosticTests.test: ELANE gene sequencing (Sanger or NGS panel)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the genetic diagnosis; identifies pathogenic ELANE mutations in >90% of CN cases; distinguishes CN from SCN and other neutropenias
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood for DNA; 3-4 weeks for results; cascade testing available for family members

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy (during nadir)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Shows maturation arrest at myelocyte stage; not routinely required but performed if diagnosis is uncertain or to exclude SCN
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Iliac crest biopsy under sedation or anesthesia; results in 5-7 days

diagnosticTests.test: C-reactive protein and inflammatory markers during nadir
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated CRP during febrile nadirs supports infection; helps distinguish true infection from cytokine-mediated fever
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test during fever; results within hours

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacterial pathogens during neutropenic sepsis; guides antibiotic selection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Aerobic and anaerobic culture; results in 48-72 hours for definitive identification

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes autoimmune neutropenia (positive ANCA or antineutrophil antibodies) which can mimic CN but lacks the regular cyclical pattern
Excludes megaloblastic anaemia as a cause of neutropenia; B12 deficiency can cause pseudoneutropenia with hypersegmented neutrophils
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests; results within 24-48 hours

diagnosticTests.test: Flow cytometry for neutrophil chemotaxis and oxidative burst (optional)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents functional neutrophil abnormalities in CN; neutrophils have impaired chemotaxis and bactericidal function even during recovery phase
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialised immunology laboratory; results in 1-2 weeks

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of CN management is subcutaneous G-CSF (filgrastim 2-5 µg/kg 2-3 times per week, or lenograstim), which raises the neutrophil nadir above the infection-risk threshold and reduces infection frequency. Daily G-CSF may be needed during active infections. Antimicrobial prophylaxis with oral ciprofloxacin 250-500mg daily during the week before each expected nadir significantly reduces bacterial infections in patients with frequent febrile episodes. For oral ulcer management, topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone orabase) and chlorhexidine mouthwash are first-line. Antipyretics (paracetamol, ibuprofen) and rest during nadirs help manage symptoms. HSCT is reserved for severe, G-CSF-unresponsive cases. Gene therapy for ELANE CN is under preclinical investigation. Management is coordinated by a haematologist with expertise in neutrophil disorders; patients should have an emergency management plan for febrile neutropenia.

medicalTreatments.name: Filgrastim (recombinant human G-CSF) 2-5 µg/kg subcutaneously 2-3 times per week
medicalTreatments.description: Standard prophylactic treatment for CN; raises neutrophil count throughout the cycle and reduces infection frequency and severity. Dose adjusted to maintain ANC >1,000/µL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces infection rate by 80-90%; transforms the disease from unpredictable to manageable; improves quality of life dramatically

medicalTreatments.name: Lenograstim (glycosylated G-CSF) 2-5 µg/kg subcutaneously 2-3 times per week
medicalTreatments.description: Glycosylated formulation of G-CSF with potentially longer half-life; used equivalently to filgrastim.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to filgrastim; some patients prefer the less painful injection volume

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic prophylaxis (ciprofloxacin 250-500mg daily during nadir week)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral fluoroquinolone prophylaxis during the week before expected nadir to reduce bacterial infection rate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces febrile episodes and documented bacterial infections by 50-60%; most useful in patients not fully controlled on G-CSF alone

medicalTreatments.name: Valacyclovir 500mg twice daily for HSV prophylaxis (if applicable)
medicalTreatments.description: For patients with recurrent oral HSV lesions during nadirs; suppresses viral reactivation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents HSV reactivation and associated oral ulceration in 70-80%

medicalTreatments.name: Topical triamcinolone orabase for oral ulcers
medicalTreatments.description: High-potency corticosteroid applied to aphthous ulcers 2-4 times daily to reduce pain and duration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces ulcer pain and healing time by 2-3 days; does not prevent new ulcers from forming

medicalTreatments.name: Chlorhexidine 0.12% mouthwash twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Antiseptic mouthwash for oral hygiene and prevention of gingival infections during nadir.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces oral bacterial load and secondary infection of oral ulcers; prevents gingivitis

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation (if iron deficiency present)
medicalTreatments.description: For CN patients with iron deficiency anaemia from chronic infection and reduced intake; oral ferrous sulfate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects iron deficiency; improves fatigue and exercise tolerance

medicalTreatments.name: Immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG or SCIG) for patients with concurrent antibody deficiency
medicalTreatments.description: For CN patients with combined neutrophil defect and hypogammaglobulinaemia; replaces functional antibodies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces infection frequency in patients with documented hypogammaglobulinaemia; not universally indicated

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage for skin abscesses or boils
surgicalOptions.description: Required for fluctuant abscesses that do not resolve with antibiotics alone; provides source control and relieves pain.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure in 90%+ with antibiotics; promotes faster resolution

surgicalOptions.name: Dental extraction for necrotic or infected teeth
surgicalOptions.description: May be required for severe dental infections not responding to antibiotics; performed during neutrophil recovery phase with G-CSF cover.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful in preventing spread of dental infection; healing normal in recovery phase

surgicalOptions.name: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (for severe, refractory cases)
surgicalOptions.description: HSCT can cure CN by replacing the haematopoietic stem cell compartment with normal donor cells; reserved for patients with severe, G-CSF-unresponsive disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure in 80-90% with matched sibling or unrelated donor; high procedural risk; considered only for severe refractory cases

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-dose oral corticosteroids during nadir (prednisone 5-10mg)
alternativeTreatments.description: Short course of low-dose steroids at nadir onset may reduce inflammation and fever; not standard due to long-term toxicity.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces fever and inflammation in some patients; not recommended for chronic use

alternativeTreatments.name: Lithium carbonate
alternativeTreatments.description: Historical treatment that stimulates granulopoiesis; used rarely in CN unresponsive to G-CSF.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in a minority of patients; largely superseded by G-CSF

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin supplementation (B12, folate, vitamin D)
alternativeTreatments.description: Ensure adequate micronutrient status for optimal haematopoiesis; particularly important in children with recurrent infections.

whySeeSpecialist: CN requires specialist haematology management to establish the diagnosis with serial blood counts, initiate and monitor G-CSF therapy, manage febrile neutropenia emergencies, and coordinate with immunology, dental, and infectious disease specialists. A haematologist with neutrophil disorder expertise is essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a haematology centre with experience in neutrophil disorders and access to G-CSF prescribing, serial monitoring, and emergency protocols. Ask about their CN volume, G-CSF dosing approach, and infection prevention protocols.
hospitalCriteria: Haematology service with neutrophil disorder expertise
Ability to perform serial CBC monitoring (home or outpatient)
Emergency department with neutrophil disorder protocol
Dental service with haematology collaboration for oral management
keyFacilities: Haematology day unit for G-CSF administration and monitoring
Home nursing service for G-CSF injections
preventionStrategies: Prophylactic antibiotics (ciprofloxacin) during the week before each expected nadir reduces febrile episodes and documented bacterial infections.
G-CSF prophylaxis transforms the disease; maintaining regular G-CSF reduces infection burden and prevents organ damage from recurrent infections.
Good oral hygiene with chlorhexidine mouthwash reduces oral ulcer severity and secondary infection.
Annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination reduce respiratory infections that can trigger severe nadirs.
Avoidance of live attenuated vaccines during severe neutropenic nadirs.
lifestyleModifications: Learn to recognise the prodromal symptoms of each nadir (fatigue, headache, oral tingling) and initiate sick-day preparations (rest, hydration, infection precautions).
Plan major activities, travel, and events around the cycle once it is well characterised.
Avoid contact with people who have active infections during the week before the nadir and during the nadir itself.
Maintain excellent dental hygiene with regular dental review every 6 months.
Carry a medical alert card identifying CN and the emergency management plan for febrile neutropenia.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to support immune function and recovery from infections
Iron-rich foods or supplementation if iron deficient from chronic infection
Balanced diet with fruit, vegetables, and omega-3 fatty acids to support overall health and reduce inflammation
Small frequent meals during nadir to maintain caloric intake when appetite is reduced
avoid: Raw or undercooked foods during neutropenic nadirs (increased infection risk)
Grapefruit juice with ciprofloxacin (reduces fluoroquinolone absorption)
Excessive alcohol (impairs bone marrow function and immune response)
Foods that can trigger oral ulcers (sharp, acidic, spicy foods) during nadir

exerciseGuidelines: Light exercise is encouraged during recovery phases. During nadirs, rest is recommended as fatigue and malaise limit activity. Gradual return to exercise as neutrophil count recovers. Swimming is acceptable during recovery phases but avoid public pools during nadirs due to infection risk.
dailyManagement: Administer G-CSF 2-3 times per week on a consistent schedule; mark the calendar to maintain the routine.
Track symptoms and approximate nadir timing in a symptom diary; after 6-8 cycles, the pattern should be clear enough to predict upcoming nadirs.
Begin prophylactic antibiotics (ciprofloxacin) 5-7 days before the expected nadir.
Report fever above 38.5°C during any phase to the haematology team; have an emergency plan for hospital admission.
Maintain excellent oral hygiene with chlorhexidine mouthwash twice daily.
Attend all dental review appointments every 6 months.
prognosis: With modern G-CSF therapy, the prognosis of CN is excellent. Most patients achieve near-normal quality of life with infection rates reduced by 80-90%. Life expectancy is normal with appropriate management. The main determinants of outcome are: early diagnosis to prevent organ damage from recurrent infections; consistent G-CSF prophylaxis adherence; and prompt management of febrile episodes during nadirs. Unlike SCN, CN does not carry a significantly elevated risk of progression to MDS or AML. G-CSF dose requirements are stable throughout life, unlike SCN where dose escalation is required over time. Pregnancy is safe in CN with continued G-CSF support.
recoveryTimeline: CN is a lifelong condition with no cure (except HSCT for severe refractory cases). G-CSF prophylaxis is required indefinitely, with most patients continuing therapy throughout life. The cycle is characteristically stable within individuals over decades; some elderly patients report gradual attenuation of nadir severity. Each G-CSF injection raises neutrophil counts within 4-6 hours, with peak effect at 24-48 hours.
complications: Febrile neutropenia with serious bacterial infection (sepsis, pneumonia, cellulitis) — managed with urgent IV antibiotics and G-CSF support; prevented with regular G-CSF prophylaxis.
Chronic gingivitis and periodontitis from recurrent oral infections — managed with dental hygiene, chlorhexidine, and regular dental review.
Persistent oral ulceration causing pain, difficulty eating, and weight loss — managed with topical steroids and G-CSF.
Recurrent otitis media with hearing loss — requires ENT review, tympanostomy tubes in children, and hearing assessment.
Chronic fatigue from recurrent infection and inflammatory cytokine release — improved with effective G-CSF prophylaxis.
Splenomegaly from chronic immune activation — monitor for hypersplenism and rupture risk.
Growth impairment in children from chronic infection and inflammatory load — G-CSF improves growth trajectory.
Progression to myelodysplastic syndrome or AML is extremely rare in CN (unlike SCN) but requires monitoring with annual CBC.

supportResources.name: Neutropenia Support Association of Canada
supportResources.description: Patient organization providing education, support, and advocacy for people with neutropenia including CN.

supportResources.name: Severe Chronic Neutropenia International Registry (SCNIR)
supportResources.description: International registry and research organisation for severe chronic neutropenia including CN; supports patients and advances research.

supportResources.name: The Royal Marsden Neutropenia Service (UK)
supportResources.description: UK specialist centre for neutropenia management and research.

supportResources.name: NORD — National Organization for Rare Disorders (US)
supportResources.description: US rare disease organisation with information on CN and patient support resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Severe Congenital Neutropenia (SCN)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SCN has persistently severe neutropenia (ANC <200/µL) without the cyclical pattern of CN. SCN is also caused by ELANE mutations but with different mutation types and more severe phenotype. SCN has a significant risk of progression to MDS/AML; CN does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Autoimmune Neutropenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Autoimmune neutropenia results from anti-neutrophil antibodies destroying neutrophils; it may fluctuate but lacks the precise 21-day periodicity of CN. Autoantibody testing and serial counts distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Benign Ethnic Neutropenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: BEN is a normal variant in people of African, Middle Eastern, or West African descent with consistently low neutrophil counts (500-1,000/µL) but no symptoms, infections, or cyclical pattern. No treatment is needed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Drug-Induced Neutropenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Drug-induced neutropenia has a temporal relationship to a specific medication (clozapine, antithyroid drugs, NSAIDs); stopping the drug usually leads to recovery. CN has a regular cyclical pattern unrelated to medication.

relatedConditions.name: Severe Congenital Neutropenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both SCN and CN are caused by ELANE mutations but with different clinical patterns and prognosis. SCN has persistent neutropenia and MDS/AML risk; CN has cyclical neutropenia without malignant transformation risk.

relatedConditions.name: Autoimmune Neutropenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune neutropenia presents similarly to CN with recurrent infections but lacks the regular cycle. ANCA testing and serial counts distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Bone Marrow Failure Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: CN is part of the bone marrow failure spectrum; the cyclical oscillator defect represents a specific failure of haematopoietic regulation.

faqs.question: What is cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Cyclic neutropenia is a rare genetic disorder causing neutrophil counts to rise and fall in a predictable cycle of approximately 21 days. During each nadir, neutrophil counts fall severely, increasing susceptibility to bacterial infections, oral ulcers, and fever.

faqs.question: Is cyclic neutropenia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. CN is autosomal dominant, caused by mutations in the ELANE gene. An affected parent has a 50% chance of passing the mutation to each child. Approximately 50% of cases are inherited and 50% are de novo mutations.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Symptoms cluster during neutropenic nadirs and include fever, oral ulcers, gingivitis, skin infections (boils, cellulitis), upper respiratory infections, fatigue, and malaise. Between nadirs, most patients are well with normal neutrophil counts.

faqs.question: How is cyclic neutropenia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires serial complete blood counts (2-3x per week for 6-8 weeks) to demonstrate the cyclical neutrophil pattern. Genetic testing for ELANE mutations confirms the diagnosis. Most patients are diagnosed in childhood.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Subcutaneous G-CSF (filgrastim) 2-5 µg/kg 2-3 times per week is the standard treatment. It raises neutrophil counts throughout the cycle and dramatically reduces infections. Antibiotic prophylaxis during nadir weeks may also be used.

faqs.question: What is G-CSF for cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: G-CSF (granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, filgrastim) stimulates the bone marrow to produce more neutrophils. Regular subcutaneous injections transform the unpredictable cycle into a manageable condition with dramatically fewer infections.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Life expectancy is normal with appropriate management. G-CSF prophylaxis has transformed outcomes; most patients live full, active lives with minimal interruption from infections.

faqs.question: How often does the cycle occur in cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: The cycle typically repeats every 21 days (range 14-35 days). Each nadir lasts 3-5 days, after which the neutrophil count recovers over 5-7 days before the cycle repeats. The cycle is usually consistent within individuals over decades.

faqs.question: Can cyclic neutropenia be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for CN apart from haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (reserved for severe refractory cases). Lifelong G-CSF prophylaxis manages the condition effectively. Gene therapy approaches are under investigation.

faqs.question: Does cyclic neutropenia increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Unlike severe congenital neutropenia (SCN), CN does not carry a significantly elevated risk of progression to MDS or AML. Regular monitoring is still recommended, but the malignant transformation risk is very low.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cyclic neutropenia and severe congenital neutropenia?
faqs.answer: CN has cyclical neutropenia (neutrophils rise and fall in a 21-day pattern); SCN has persistently low neutrophils without cycles. Both are caused by ELANE mutations but have different mutation types. SCN has a significant MDS/AML risk; CN does not.

faqs.question: Can someone with cyclic neutropenia have children?
faqs.answer: Yes. CN patients can have children normally. There is a 50% chance of passing the ELANE mutation to each child. Pregnancy management involves continued G-CSF, specialist obstetric care, and planning for predictable nadirs around delivery.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested for cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Yes. First-degree relatives should be offered ELANE genetic testing and serial CBC monitoring to identify affected individuals who can benefit from early G-CSF treatment.

faqs.question: What infections occur in cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: During nadirs, patients are susceptible to bacterial infections including skin and soft tissue infections (boils, cellulitis), respiratory infections (sinusitis, otitis media, pneumonia), and perirectal infections. Fungal infections are less common.

faqs.question: How do you know when a nadir is coming?
faqs.answer: Most patients develop prodromal symptoms 1-2 days before each nadir: fatigue, headache, oral tingling, malaise. After tracking the cycle for several months, patients can predict upcoming nadirs accurately enough to plan activities.

faqs.question: Is cyclic neutropenia the same as neutropenia from chemotherapy?
faqs.answer: No. Chemotherapy-induced neutropenia is secondary to toxic destruction of bone marrow cells. CN is a primary genetic disorder of the haematopoietic oscillator. Both produce severe neutropenia but have different causes and management.

faqs.question: Can vaccination trigger a nadir in cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Live vaccines should be avoided during severe neutropenic nadirs. Killed vaccines are generally safe but may cause a brief, mild nadir by stimulating the immune system. Discuss timing with the haematologist.

faqs.question: What causes oral ulcers in cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Oral ulcers (aphthous stomatitis) in CN result from neutropenia allowing oral bacterial overgrowth and mucosal inflammation. They are painful and may become secondarily infected. Topical steroids and chlorhexidine help manage them.

faqs.question: Does cyclic neutropenia affect growth in children?
faqs.answer: Children with poorly controlled CN may have impaired growth due to chronic infection and inflammation. Effective G-CSF prophylaxis improves growth trajectory significantly. Height often normalises with good disease control.

faqs.question: Can you travel with cyclic neutropenia?
faqs.answer: Yes, with preparation. Carry G-CSF, emergency protocols, and medical alert information. Plan travel around predictable nadirs. Avoid travel to areas with limited medical facilities. Consult the haematologist before international travel.

metaTitle: Cyclic Neutropenia: Symptoms, G-CSF Treatment & Life Management | aihealz
metaDescription: Cyclic neutropenia: ELANE mutation, 21-day cycle, G-CSF treatment, infection prevention, and prognosis. Medically reviewed guide.
